output:
# LAPSE and VERNIER: Curing the Silent Age-Gap Calibration Blind Spot in Automatic Speaker Verification with an Exact-Collapse, Target/Non-Target-Asymmetric Conditional Recalibration

## 1. Background and Current State of the Art

Deep-learning automatic speaker verification (ASV) has converged on a single dominant production pipeline. A neural speaker-embedding extractor (i-vector, x-vector, or the current state-of-the-art ECAPA-TDNN) maps a variable-length recording to a fixed-dimensional embedding [1,2,4,5]; at enrollment, target-speaker recordings are summarized into a reference template; at verification, a claimed identity is validated by computing the similarity between the enrollment template and the verification recording [1,2]. In modern systems this similarity is a single cosine-similarity SCALAR -- cosine scoring has largely supplanted earlier PLDA back-ends for strong neural embeddings [3] -- optionally refined by adaptive score normalization (adaptive s-norm / AS-Norm) against an impostor cohort [10,11,12], and the accept/reject decision is taken by thresholding that scalar at a fixed, covariate-blind operating point [13,14]. Toolkits such as SpeechBrain ship ECAPA-TDNN trained on VoxCeleb2 with cosine plus adaptive s-norm as a reference recipe, reporting representative equal error rates (EER) of about 1.28 percent on Vox1-O and 1.95 percent on Vox1-E for a standard recipe (exact figures are recipe- and version-dependent) [5,6,7,8].

ASV accuracy depends strongly on how closely the enrollment and verification CONDITIONS are matched. Prior work studies many mismatch factors (background environment, channel, duration, session) [10,12,16], and a growing line studies AGEING: the degradation of EER as the time gap between enrollment and verification grows [17,18]. The source paper analyzes this systematically across two very different languages and corpora -- VoxCeleb1-Age-Enriched (USA, English) [9] and LCFSH (Finnish) -- with separate analyses for male and female speakers, using SpeechBrain ECAPA-TDNN with cosine similarity and adaptive s-norm [5,6]. Rather than reporting only mean scores, it fits Linear Mixed-Effect (LME) models that jointly capture fixed and random effects in the ASV scores [20,21].

The LME methodology fits S_i = mu + beta * delta_i + gamma, where S_i is the ASV score of the i-th trial, mu is the intercept (mean score in a condition), delta_i is the AGE DIFFERENCE between the enrollment and verification audio, beta is the fixed-effect coefficient capturing the linear relation between age difference and score, and gamma is a random effect absorbing other (largely per-speaker) variability together with the trial-level residual [20,21]. The empirical finding is sharp and consistent: the fixed-effect coefficients are NEGATIVE, and the coefficients for TARGET scores are notably LARGER in magnitude than for NON-TARGET scores (|beta_target| >> |beta_nontarget|), so target (genuine) scores are MORE sensitive to ageing than non-target (impostor) scores. Non-target score ranges span a narrow band while target scores are distributed more widely and drift further as delta grows; the effect is well-evidenced, larger for age-groups with larger age gaps, and heterogeneous across gender and language.

Crucially, this rich body of work is DIAGNOSTIC, not corrective. The LME MEASURES beta and quantifies how EER grows with the age gap, but it never feeds that knowledge back into the decision: the deployed system still thresholds one cosine scalar at one covariate-blind operating point, exactly as before. There is a measured, structured, asymmetric ageing effect on one side of the system and a covariate-blind decision rule on the other, and nothing connects them. This gap between an excellent measurement and an unchanged decision is the opening this work targets.

## 2. Key Findings and Analysis

We name the gap LAPSE (Latent Ageing-induced, Pooled-EER-Silent score Erosion). The deployed system decides accept/reject by thresholding a single cosine-similarity SCALAR at a covariate-blind operating point, and although adaptive s-norm normalizes that scalar against an impostor COHORT, it is structurally blind to the OBSERVABLE enrollment-verification age gap delta_i (computable at trial time from recording timestamps). A single scalar judged at a fixed or s-norm-shifted threshold is representationally INCAPABLE of expressing the delta-conditional shift that ageing induces in the score distributions, a shift that is ASYMMETRIC (|beta_target| >> |beta_nontarget|, both negative) and HETEROGENEOUS across gender and language [17,18,20]. The consequence is that the operating point is miscalibrated as a FUNCTION of delta, so genuine speakers with large enrollment-verification age gaps suffer a monotonically rising false-reject rate.

The defining property of LAPSE is that the loss is SILENT under pooled EER: because the pooled metric marginalizes over delta, a system whose false-reject rate is climbing steeply for large-age-gap genuine users can still report a flat, healthy pooled EER, so operators watching the standard dashboard see nothing wrong [13,14,15]. The harm SCALES with the age gap delta, with gender, and with language (more error in larger-gap, higher-asymmetry cells), and it VANISHES if and only if the delta-conditional target and non-target shift/scale maps are constant, i.e. beta_target = beta_nontarget = 0 (no ageing drift). LAPSE thus names precisely the uncorrected, metric-invisible erosion that the source paper measures but does not fix.

We introduce VERNIER, an age-gap-conditioned fine recalibration of the operating point that turns the measured beta into a correction. VERNIER operates purely at the SCORE/decision layer (no extractor retraining): for each class c in {target, non-target} it fits a class-conditional affine map z_c = (s - mu_c(delta, g)) / sigma_c(delta, g) on the observable covariate delta (and optionally gender/language g), yielding a delta-conditional threshold tau(delta, g). Because target and non-target distributions age at different rates, fitting SEPARATE maps is essential -- a single additive shift cannot calibrate both simultaneously. The maps are estimated by HIERARCHICAL / PARTIAL-POOLING (LME-style random effects gamma), reusing the source paper's S = mu + beta * delta + gamma structure to CALIBRATE rather than merely describe, borrowing strength into data-poor large-delta bins [16,20,21,25,26,27]. The deployed global threshold is the coarse main scale and VERNIER adds an age-gap-conditioned fine correction on top of it.

The decisive design property is the EXACT-COLLAPSE guarantee. Under the covariate-blind identity default -- clamping the conditional shift/scale maps to constants, so the vernier reads zero -- VERNIER reproduces the deployed adaptive-s-norm plus global-threshold decision DECISION-FOR-DECISION for every trial, with collapse error E_collapse <= 1e-6 enforced by a CI-gateable build check [13,14]. This is a strict, safe generalization: zero regression at delta = 0 and on pooled EER, and turning the new machinery on can only add corrections the baseline misses. The novelty wedge is four clauses that no prior line covers more than two of: C1 operates at the deployed SCORE/decision layer (no embedding-extractor retraining); C2 conditions calibration on the OBSERVABLE enrollment-verification age gap delta; C3 models the TARGET vs NON-TARGET ageing ASYMMETRY with separate conditional maps (not a single additive beta * delta shift); C4 provably EXACT-collapses to the deployed adaptive-s-norm plus global-threshold baseline at the identity default.

## 3. Novel Research Idea (5-Question Formulation)

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Can replacing automatic speaker verification's covariate-blind cosine threshold with an observable age-gap-conditional calibration that fits separate target and non-target maps reduce false rejects for large-age-gap genuine users while provably collapsing, decision-for-decision, to the deployed adaptive-s-norm global-threshold baseline at the identity default with zero pooled-EER regression?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

P1 - A latent vulnerability in the dominant production ASV pattern. ASV is a high-stakes, widely deployed biometric: retail/telephone banking, contact-center caller authentication, law-enforcement and forensic voice comparison, healthcare/remote patient ID [7,8,15]. The dominant production rule is the one this spine targets: extract a fixed-dim embedding (i-vector/x-vector/ECAPA-TDNN) [2,4,5], compute a single cosine-similarity SCALAR vs the enrollment template, optionally adaptive-s-norm it against an impostor cohort [10,11,12], accept/reject at a covariate-blind operating point [13,14]. Because this one pipeline is universal, any structural blind spot in it is a BROAD latent vulnerability across millions of decisions. LAPSE identifies exactly such a blind spot: the scalar-at-a-threshold decision cannot express the delta-conditional distributional shift ageing induces, and s-norm's cohort normalization is blind to the observable enrollment-verification age gap delta.

P2 - The failure is silent and metric-invisible. Pooled EER marginalizes over delta, so a system whose FRR is climbing steeply for large-age-gap genuine users still reports a flat, healthy pooled EER [13,14,15]. Operators measuring the wrong aggregate conclude nothing is wrong while a specific subpopulation is increasingly locked out. The source-paper LME [18,20] makes the mechanism precise: ageing imposes an ASYMMETRIC shift (|beta_target| >> |beta_nontarget|, both negative), heterogeneous across gender/language; pooling hides exactly this. A vulnerability you cannot see on your dashboard is one you will never fix unaided.

P3 - Fairness and disparate impact along an age axis. The harmed users are not random: long-tenure and re-enrolled-after-years populations - customers enrolled a decade ago, children whose voices matured, elderly users with shifted phonation [16,17,18]. Often the most loyal or most vulnerable customers, systematically disadvantaged by an unchanged threshold whose pooled EER betrays nothing. Age is protected-attribute-adjacent; monotonic FRR growth with the age gap is textbook DISPARATE IMPACT while the headline metric passes [22,23], connecting to demographic-bias-in-voice-biometrics work [22,23].

P4 - Deployability: ship-in-shadow with provably zero single-trial regression. VERNIER operates purely at the SCORE/decision layer (no extractor retraining) and EXACT-collapses to the shipped adaptive-s-norm + global-threshold baseline at the covariate-blind identity default (E_collapse <= 1e-6, CI-gateable). That collapse is the deployability unlock: enable in shadow mode, validate decision-for-decision reproduction at delta=0, then dial up on large-delta bins with a provable zero-regression guarantee. This is an adoptable component, not just an analysis.

P5 - Generality: a covariate-conditional-calibration lens. The core move - separate target vs non-target conditional LLR maps on an observable, RELATIONAL mismatch covariate, with hierarchical partial pooling into data-poor bins [16,20,21] - is a general recipe extending to other enrollment-verification mismatch covariates (channel/device, session, language, transient health) [16] and other ageing biometrics (face/fingerprint). It operationalizes the source paper's diagnostic: it turns the measured beta from a passive statistic into a quantitative, falsifiable correction whose lift scales with delta x ageing-asymmetry and vanishes exactly when the asymmetry vanishes.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

O1 (BINDING) - ASYMMETRY DEFEATS A NAIVE ADDITIVE CORRECTION. Ageing shifts target and non-target distributions at different rates (|beta_target| >> |beta_nontarget|, both negative), so a single additive correction of the form s - beta * delta cannot recalibrate both classes at once: any shift large enough to recenter genuine scores over-moves the narrow non-target band and inflates the false-accept rate. The obstacle is REPRESENTATIONAL, not a tuning issue -- it forces SEPARATE class-conditional maps for target and non-target, which is exactly clause C3 and the reason simpler corrections structurally fail.

O2 - IDENTIFIABILITY / DATA SPARSITY UNDER A LARGE RANDOM EFFECT gamma. The large-delta bins where the correction matters most are the most data-poor, and the per-speaker random effect gamma is large relative to the fixed ageing effect beta, so a naive per-bin fit is unidentifiable and overfits. The obstacle is REPRESENTATIONAL: it requires a hierarchical / partial-pooling estimator that borrows strength across bins and speakers [20,21,27] rather than more data alone.

O3 - DISENTANGLING AGEING FROM CHANNEL/SESSION MISMATCH. The observable age gap delta is correlated with recording-era, device, and session changes, so a delta-conditional map can silently absorb channel drift and mis-attribute it to ageing, corrupting the correction and its generalization. The obstacle is REPRESENTATIONAL: the calibration must include channel/quality nuisance controls and within-speaker contrasts so that the delta dimension isolates ageing rather than confounds [16].

O4 - FAIRNESS BACKFIRE FROM CONDITIONING ON AGE/GENDER. Conditioning the operating point on age-adjacent and gender covariates can itself create disparate operating points and new group harms, turning a fairness fix into a fairness risk. The obstacle is REPRESENTATIONAL: the method must carry an explicit equal-FRR-across-delta-and-group objective and the identity-default safety net, not merely minimize an aggregate loss [22,23].

O5 - METRIC BLINDNESS. The standard pooled EER cannot see either the harm or the fix, so optimizing or reporting it provides no signal and no falsification path. The obstacle is REPRESENTATIONAL: progress requires delta-stratified evaluation -- per-bin actual-Cllr minus min-Cllr and per-bin FRR at fixed FAR -- as first-class metrics [13,14], without which the contribution is unmeasurable.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

The wedge needs four clauses jointly: C1 score/decision layer (no retraining); C2 condition on observable age gap delta; C3 separate target/non-target asymmetric maps (not a single additive beta * delta); C4 exact collapse to adaptive-s-norm + global threshold. No prior line covers more than two.

Near-miss 1 - Adaptive s-norm / AS-Norm (Matejka et al. 2017; Cumani et al. 2011) [11,12]. Covers C1. Misses C2 (normalizes vs impostor cohort, not delta), C3 (one symmetric shift), C4 (it is the baseline VERNIER collapses to).

Near-miss 2 - Quality-Measure-Function / quality-aware LLR calibration (Mandasari, Saeidi, McLaren, van Leeuwen, IEEE TASLP 2013) [16]. Covers C1 + a C2-adjacent conditioning. Misses C3 (single shared map) and C4 (no exact collapse). Its covariates are per-utterance quality scalars; delta is a RELATIONAL between-recording covariate QMF was never designed to consume.

Near-miss 3 - Age-invariant / age-adversarial embeddings (Qin et al., Interspeech 2022; Zhang et al., Interspeech 2024) [19,24]. Addresses the C2/C3 phenomenon conceptually. Misses C1 (extractor-level, needs retraining + age labels), C3 in the precise sense (single age-invariant space, not separate decision maps), C4 (no decision-for-decision collapse). It changes the representation; VERNIER changes the decision.

Near-miss 4 - Diagnostic ageing LME / longitudinal studies (the source paper; Kelly, Drygajlo & Harte, ICB 2012; Kelly, Saeidi, Harte & van Leeuwen, Interspeech 2014) [17,18]. MEASURES beta/EER growth (grounds C2/C3 as diagnosis). Misses C1 and C4 - misses method entirely: no score-layer correction, no separate maps, no collapse.

Crisp summary: VERNIER is the first to do C1+C2+C3+C4 together - a score-layer, delta-conditional, target/non-target-asymmetric calibration that exact-collapses to the deployed adaptive-s-norm global-threshold baseline.

Coverage gaps: (a) no relational-covariate calibration (existing quality calibration conditions on per-utterance scalars, not a between-recording mismatch covariate) [16]; (b) no safe-generalization / exact-collapse guarantees in covariate-conditional ASV [13,14]; (c) fairness work stops at measurement, offering no operating-point correction that closes the age-gap FRR disparity while leaving pooled EER untouched [22,23].

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

Q5a - METHOD. Base = SpeechBrain ECAPA-TDNN trained on VoxCeleb2, cosine + adaptive s-norm, no retraining [5,6]; delta = enrollment-verification age gap from timestamps. VERNIER = separate class-conditional affine maps z_c = (s - mu_c(delta, g)) / sigma_c(delta, g) for c in {tar, non}, yielding a delta-conditional threshold tau(delta, g). Estimation is by a partial-pooling LME hierarchy that reuses S = mu + beta * delta + gamma to CALIBRATE rather than describe, with channel/quality nuisance controls [16,20,21,25,26,27]; when gender/language labels are unavailable or undesirable, VERNIER defaults to delta-only conditioning so no sensitive attribute is required at inference. EXACT-COLLAPSE: clamping the maps to constants makes the decision equal to adaptive-s-norm + global threshold for every trial, with E_collapse <= 1e-6 enforced by a CI gate [13,14].

Q5b - DATASETS. VoxCeleb1-Age-Enriched (English/USA) [7,9]; LCFSH (Finnish, longitudinal); cross-corpus generalization on TCDSA [Kelly et al., 17] or a NIST SRE longitudinal subset [15], held-out only. SPEAKER-disjoint splits plus a held-out-delta extrapolation protocol; delta bins 0, (0,2], (2,5], (5,10], >10 years.

Q5c - METRICS. delta-stratified EER plus a pooled-EER regression check; minDCF per bin; actual-Cllr and min-Cllr [13]; PRIMARY = calibration loss (actual-Cllr minus min-Cllr) per bin; per-bin FRR at fixed FAR; fairness = max EER/FRR gap across delta and gender x language; E_collapse <= 1e-6.

Q5d - BASELINES and ABLATIONS. Baselines: B1 global threshold; B2 adaptive s-norm (the collapse target); B3 naive additive s - beta * delta (predicted to inflate non-target FAR); B4 QMF / quality-aware delta-blind [16]; B5 VERNIER. Ablations: shared vs separate maps; pooled vs partial-pooling; delta-only vs delta+gender+language; affine vs monotone-nonparametric.

Q5e - VALIDATION. A hard EXACT-COLLAPSE CI gate (build fails if violated) plus three falsifiability triples: (1) the collapse identity; (2) large-delta FRR at fixed FAR vs B2; (3) no pooled-EER / delta ~= 0 regression and no non-target FAR inflation vs B3.

Q5f - EXPECTED OUTCOMES (as HYPOTHESES). E_collapse <= 1e-6 by construction; large-delta FRR at fixed FAR relative reduction ~= 15-40 percent in the >10yr bin, tapering to 0 as delta -> 0; calibration-loss reduction ~= 30-60 percent concentrated in large-delta bins, with min-Cllr roughly unchanged; reduced max EER/FRR gap; pooled and delta ~= 0 EER change ~= 0 within an equivalence CI; B3 inflates non-target FAR, empirically demonstrating O1.

Q5g - Responsible-AI and deployment considerations. VoxCeleb and LCFSH are used under their respective dataset licenses and terms, and the findings are grounded in only two languages (English, Finnish) and a binary gender taxonomy, so generalization to other demographics and to non-binary gender requires further study. When gender/language labels are unavailable or undesirable, VERNIER defaults to delta-only conditioning, so no sensitive attribute is required at inference. For fairness evaluation we report a full disaggregated per-(delta-bin x gender x language) FRR table as a primary metric, not only the scalar max-gap across groups [22,23]. In forensic, law-enforcement, banking, and healthcare contexts VERNIER is intended to inform a human reviewer and must not be the sole arbiter of an accept/reject decision. As a privacy-by-design and data-minimization principle, delta is derived purely from recording timestamps without storing additional sensitive attributes. Finally, deploying organizations should provide an accessible human-review appeal path so that users harmed by a wrong reject have a clear route to contest and remediate the outcome.

## 4. References and Evidence

[1] D. A. Reynolds, T. F. Quatieri, R. B. Dunn, "Speaker Verification Using Adapted Gaussian Mixture Models," Digital Signal Processing, 2000.
[2] N. Dehak, P. J. Kenny, R. Dehak, P. Dumouchel, P. Ouellet, "Front-End Factor Analysis for Speaker Verification," IEEE TASLP, 2011.
[3] S. J. D. Prince, J. H. Elder, "Probabilistic Linear Discriminant Analysis for Inferences About Identity," ICCV, 2007.
[4] D. Snyder, D. Garcia-Romero, G. Sell, D. Povey, S. Khudanpur, "X-Vectors: Robust DNN Embeddings for Speaker Recognition," ICASSP, 2018.
[5] B. Desplanques, J. Thienpondt, K. Demuynck, "ECAPA-TDNN: Emphasized Channel Attention, Propagation and Aggregation in TDNN Based Speaker Verification," Interspeech, 2020.
[6] M. Ravanelli, T. Parcollet, P. Plantinga, A. Rouhe, S. Cornell, et al., "SpeechBrain: A General-Purpose Speech Toolkit," arXiv:2106.04624, 2021.
[7] A. Nagrani, J. S. Chung, A. Zisserman, "VoxCeleb: A Large-Scale Speaker Identification Dataset," Interspeech, 2017.
[8] J. S. Chung, A. Nagrani, A. Zisserman, "VoxCeleb2: Deep Speaker Recognition," Interspeech, 2018.
[9] K. Hechmi, T. N. Trong, V. Hautamaki, T. Kinnunen, "VoxCeleb Enrichment for Age and Gender Recognition," IEEE ASRU, 2021.
[10] R. Auckenthaler, M. Carey, H. Lloyd-Thomas, "Score Normalization for Text-Independent Speaker Verification Systems," Digital Signal Processing, 2000.
[11] P. Matejka, O. Novotny, O. Plchot, L. Burget, M. Diez, J. Cernocky, "Analysis of Score Normalization in Multilingual Speaker Recognition," Interspeech, 2017.
[12] S. Cumani, P. D. Batzu, D. Colibro, C. Vair, P. Laface, V. Vasilakakis, "Comparison of Speaker Recognition Approaches for Real Applications," Interspeech, 2011.
[13] N. Brummer, J. du Preez, "Application-Independent Evaluation of Speaker Detection," Computer Speech & Language, 2006.
[14] D. A. van Leeuwen, N. Brummer, "An Introduction to Application-Independent Evaluation of Speaker Recognition Systems," Speaker Classification I, LNCS, Springer, 2007.
[15] S. O. Sadjadi, C. Greenberg, E. Singer, D. Reynolds, L. Mason, J. Hernandez-Cordero, "The 2018 NIST Speaker Recognition Evaluation," Interspeech, 2019.
[16] M. I. Mandasari, R. Saeidi, M. McLaren, D. A. van Leeuwen, "Quality Measure Functions for Calibration of Speaker Recognition Systems in Various Duration Conditions," IEEE TASLP, 2013.
[17] F. Kelly, A. Drygajlo, N. Harte, "Speaker Verification with Long-Term Ageing Data," IAPR ICB, 2012.
[18] F. Kelly, R. Saeidi, N. Harte, D. A. van Leeuwen, "Effect of Long-Term Ageing on i-Vector Speaker Verification," Interspeech, 2014.
[19] X. Qin, N. Li, C. Weng, D. Su, M. Li, "Cross-Age Speaker Verification: Learning Age-Invariant Speaker Embeddings," Interspeech, 2022.
[20] D. Bates, M. Machler, B. Bolker, S. Walker, "Fitting Linear Mixed-Effects Models Using lme4," Journal of Statistical Software, 2015.
[21] R. H. Baayen, D. J. Davidson, D. M. Bates, "Mixed-Effects Modeling with Crossed Random Effects for Subjects and Items," Journal of Memory and Language, 2008.
[22] G. Fenu, M. Marras, G. Medda, G. Meloni, "Fair Voice Biometrics: Impact of Demographic Imbalance on Group Fairness in Speaker Recognition," Interspeech, 2021.
[23] W. T. Hutiri, A. Y. Ding, "Bias in Automated Speaker Recognition," ACM FAccT, 2022.
[24] F. Zhang, et al., "Disentangling Age and Identity with a Mutual Information Minimization Approach for Cross-Age Speaker Verification," Interspeech, 2024.
[25] J. Platt, "Probabilistic Outputs for Support Vector Machines and Comparisons to Regularized Likelihood Methods," Advances in Large Margin Classifiers, 1999/2000.
[26] B. Zadrozny, C. Elkan, "Transforming Classifier Scores into Accurate Multiclass Probability Estimates," ACM SIGKDD (KDD), 2002.
[27] A. Gelman, J. Hill, "Data Analysis Using Regression and Multilevel/Hierarchical Models," Cambridge University Press, 2007.
